text: Acadia National Park
day. morning sun sheds light
on the North American continent. It falls first on the craggy coast of the state of Maine
and Acadia, the country's most easterly national park.
A new day is being born. A new life.
In the dawn's hush, people are welcoming the first light.
All that is Acadia. The clean salt air.
The lighthouses. The miles of rocky Atlantic coast.
The verdant forests, fragrant with a scent of pine and spruce.
The cool, clear lakes. And the mountains.
Worn smooth by restless glaciers. A perfect setting for Acadia National Park.
Located on the rocky coast of down east Maine, the park is home to the tallest mountain on the U .S. Atlantic coast.
All of the reaches of this extraordinary national park begin here,
on Mount Desert Island, Arden headquarters of Acadia.
The name desert is French, pronounced Douzere, Île de Mont Douzere.
In this sense, Douzere does not mean a desert island, but rather an island that is deserted, a lonely island.
Well, it isn't lonely anymore. Samuel de Champlain, the French explorer,
gave the island its name in 1604, after his ship crashed into a shoal.
In time, it became home to a scattering of hardy New Englanders.
Farmers, lumbermen, shipbuilders, and fishermen plumbing the Atlantic for lobster.
a new breed began filtering in, artists of the Hudson River School of Painting.
Thomas Cole Frederick Church
Their renderings of beautiful Acadia appeared on magazine covers across the nation.
But no artist's palette can do justice to the autumn colors in Acadia National Park.
Yet it was to escape the sweltering July and August heat in New York City that the wealthiest of the wealthy began summering in Acadia.
They built storybook mansions, which they modestly called cottages.
Mount Desert became a playground for the privileged.
Afternoon teas, strolls down the woods' sylvan paths.
Now that Mount Desert Island was a place of pedigree, high society had set out to preserve it.
In time, it became Acadia National Park, America's first national park east of the Mississippi,
and the first anywhere made of entirely donated lands.
John D. Rockefeller Jr., trained at Harvard as a landscape architect, undertook the designing of Acadia's carriage roads.
He spent 20 years and $20 million on these roads and strictly forbade their use by automobiles.
Today, park visitors still enjoy these miles of carefully crafted roads,
cyclists, and passengers in horse -drawn carriages.
activities than taking tea invite today's visitors.
waters, biking, beaches, hiking,
and boating on the bay. These visitors wade at a shoreline inlet.
gushing sea water may splash as high as 40 feet with a roar like thunder.
This is the sight and sound of Acadia National Park.
